---
title: "*The Emperor of All Maladies*, ten years on"
description: "Re-reading Mukherjee's biography of cancer with actual cell biology under my belt."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [books, biology]
draft: true
---

*[Scaffold — fill in.]*

Mukherjee's *The Emperor of All Maladies* was the book that tipped me into biology. I read it years before I'd touched a pipette or learned what a kinase was, and the narrative arc — three thousand years of trying to understand a disease, told as a single story — was enough. A decade later, with actual cell biology between me and the page, the book reads differently.

## What landed first time

*[The narrative power; the human cases; the historical sweep; the willingness to sit with uncertainty. What I responded to as a curious outsider.]*

## What reads differently now

*[Knowing what oncogenes and tumour suppressors actually do; understanding the molecular biology under the chemotherapy chapter; recognising which trial designs were primitive and which were ingenious.]*

## What's aged

*[Immunotherapy, checkpoint inhibitors, CAR-T — all post-publication. The genomics era is barely covered. The cancer landscape Mukherjee describes is recognisably the same disease, treated with substantially different tools.]*

## What's held up

*[The framing of cancer as cellular evolution; the handling of clinical-trial uncertainty; the willingness to say "we don't know."]*

## Worth re-reading?

*[Yes / no / depends — write the verdict.]*
